coredata.dc:title: Fluorophotometric determination of critical micelle concentration (CMC) of ionic and non-ionic surfactants with carbon dots via Stokes shift
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Lavkush Bhaisare, Mukesh
Pandey, Sunil
Shahnawaz Khan, M.
Talib, Abou
Wu, Hui-Fen
coredata.dc:description: A new and facile method for the determination of critical micelle concentration (CMC) of ionic and non-ionic surfactants is proposed in this article. Carbon dots exhibited substantial fluorescence and therefore enhanced the sensitivity of this evaluation. Understanding the formation of surfactant micelles is vital for the applications of biomedicine such as drug fabrication and smart molecular vehicles in delivering therapeutic dosage to various molecular sites. The fluorescence property of carbon dots was utilized for the first time to estimate the critical micelle concentration of surfactants. The central concept of the approach is based on the Stokes shift determination of a system composed of constant amount of carbon dots with varying concentrations of ionic and non-ionic surfactants. The synthesized carbon dots were characterized by FTIR, TEM, XRD, Raman, UV, and fluorescence spectroscope. The carbon dots were excited at 280nm so as to obtain maximum emission for the Stokes shift measurement. The CMC value of cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), Triton X-100, dodecyldimethyl(3-sulfopropyl)ammonium hydroxide (SB-12) evaluated by this approach was found to be 0.98, 7.3, 0.19, and 3.5mM, respectively. The signals of spectra were assigned and explained in terms of both electron transitions between specific molecular orbital and the interaction with solvent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Critical micelle concentration
Stokes shift
Carbon dots
Surfactants
Cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide
Sodium dodecyl sulfate
Triton X-100
Dodecyldimethyl (3-sulfopropyl)ammonium hydroxide
Fluorescence spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and purification of fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots)
Determination of critical micelle concentration of surfactants
The evaluation of critical micelle concentration (CMC) for CTAB, SDS, Triton X-100, and SB-12

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of carbon dots: (A) TEM image, (B) histogram of particle diameter distribution, (C) UV–vis spectra, and (D) fluorescence spectra excited at different wavelengths.
Continued characterizations of carbon dots: (A) FTIR spectra, (B) XRD pattern, and (C) Raman spectra.

Excitation and emission fluorescence spectra, and the corresponding relationship of the Stokes shift and the concentration of surfactant CTAB (A and B), SDS (C and D), Triton X-100 (E and F), and SB-12 (G and H), respectively.
Determination of quantum yield of carbon dots with quinine sulfate as the reference.
List of CMC evaluations from some proposed protocols and the approach in this work.

row.entry: Based on Non invasive method-Time-resolved spectroscopy, steady state

head.ce:title: Fluorophotometric determination of critical micelle concentration (CMC) of ionic and non-ionic surfactants with carbon dots via Stokes shift

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University, Kaohsiung, 70, Lien-Hai Road, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, College of Pharmacy, Kaohsiung Medical University, Kaohsiung 807, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Medical Science and Technology, National Sun Yat-Sen University, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Doctoral Degree Program in Marine Biotechnology, National Sun Yat-Sen University and Academia Sinica, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Nanosciences and Nanotechnology, National Sun Yat-Sen University, 70, Lien-Hai Road, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University, Kaohsiung, 70, Lien-Hai Road, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan. Tel.: +886 7 5252000x3955; fax: +886 7 5253908.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new and facile method for the determination of critical micelle concentration (CMC) of ionic and non-ionic surfactants is proposed in this article. Carbon dots exhibited substantial fluorescence and therefore enhanced the sensitivity of this evaluation. Understanding the formation of surfactant micelles is vital for the applications of biomedicine such as drug fabrication and smart molecular vehicles in delivering therapeutic dosage to various molecular sites. The fluorescence property of carbon dots was utilized for the first time to estimate the critical micelle concentration of surfactants. The central concept of the approach is based on the Stokes shift determination of a system composed of constant amount of carbon dots with varying concentrations of ionic and non-ionic surfactants. The synthesized carbon dots were characterized by FTIR, TEM, XRD, Raman, UV, and fluorescence spectroscope. The carbon dots were excited at 280nm so as to obtain maximum emission for the Stokes shift measurement. The CMC value of cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), Triton X-100, dodecyldimethyl(3-sulfopropyl)ammonium hydroxide (SB-12) evaluated by this approach was found to be 0.98, 7.3, 0.19, and 3.5mM, respectively. The signals of spectra were assigned and explained in terms of both electron transitions between specific molecular orbital and the interaction with solvent.

ce:section.ce:para: Surfactant is a unique group of compounds containing both a hydrophobic long-chain (tail) and a hydrophilic polar group (head) in their structures [1,2]. When the concentration reaches a specific narrow range, called critical micelle concentration [3], the molecules tend to self-associate with tails aggregating inward into an assembled micellar structure which is favored thermodynamically in polar solvents [4]. The formed micelles have a center of hydrophobic (tail) moiety and an outward head (hydrophilic) moiety took place in a continuous phase generally observed in aqueous phase. In contrast, when the outer portion of micelles is comprised of tail (hydrophobic) moiety and the head moiety assembled in center of the micelles it is called as the reverse micelle which is formed in oil phase. In this study all the experiments were specifically conducted in aqueous phase. The formed micelle has been described as structure of aggregate; it has characteristically various interactions such as hydrophobic, electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bonding. These micelles comprise unique characteristics such as uniform nanosize, core-shell structure, and being stable thermodynamically in physiological conditions due to their low critical micelle concentration [5,6]. With this amphiphilic structure, surfactants have been extensively employed in applications including the fields of chemistry [7], biotechnology, and pharmacy [8]. A variety of surfactants have been utilized with their capability to enhance the permeability of drugs across biological membranes [9]. In complex systems, surfactants were widely used in membrane protein separation, crystallization, purification and stabilization. Since the physicochemical properties of solutions below and above CMC demonstrate drastic changes, such as surface tension, electrical conductivity, turbidity, osmotic pressure, density, viscosity, and light scattering [10]. Many methods have been proposed to determine the CMC of plethora of surfactants, e.g. spectrophotometry [11], colorimetric [12], potentiometry, refractometry, conductometry, capillary electrophoresis, light scattering [13], pyrene 1:3 ratio method [14], and so forth. Among these methods, spectrophotometry is a simple and reliable tool for determination of CMC. Due to the outstanding fluorescence exhibited by carbon dots (C-dots) [15], the nanodots were introduced in an attempt to enhance the sensitivity for the fluorophotometric determination of CMC.

Most of the methods reported for the determination of critical micelle concentration have several disadvantages such as low sensitivity, the necessity of dye or probe, large volume of sample, tedious experimental techniques as well as the interpretation tools required. Additionally, some methods are particularly applicable to the CMC determination of a specific surfactant which is one of the major drawbacks of those methods [16]. In the present work, in order to solve these problems, we have developed a modified technique based on the fluorescence and the Stokes shift of C-dots, which is one category of highly fluorescent materials based on carbon materials [17] to investigate the CMC of CTAB, SDS, Triton X-100, and SB-12.

C-dots are growing popular among the families of quantum dots because of the excellent optical and biomedical properties [18]. The advantages of being able to emit intense and tunable photoluminescence (PL) have made C-dots to be a favorable ingredient for the applications in the fields of catalysis and energy [19]. Additionally, carbon quantum dots demonstrated special characteristics, e.g. non-blinking, high aqueous solubility, and non-toxicity [20]. There are many established protocols for the synthesis of amorphous as well as crystalline C-dots using facile routes such as thermal cracking of organic compounds [21], micro-wave mediated synthesis [22], laser ablation of graphite [23], electro-oxidation of graphite, and oxidation of candle soot [24].

The central concept of this work is based on the Stokes shift, the difference of peak maxima in excitation and emission spectra [25], of C-dots in the presence of varying concentration of surfactants. The C-dots decorated with both positive as well as negative charged moieties were found to be efficiently interacting with charged or non-charged head group surfactants. This phenomenon leads to the exhibition of sharp Stokes shift in the presence of the surfactants. Herein, cationic cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), anionic sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), non-ionic Triton X-100, as well as zwitterionic dodecyldimethyl(3-sulfopropyl)ammonium hydroxide (SB-12) surfactants were investigated and the CMC were evaluated. Note that this zwitterionic surfactant has seldom been studied [26]. Since the zwitterionic surfactant is electrically neutral in nature, it is easily to be distinguished from others. For the determination of CMC values, the intersection of the extrapolation of curves in a graph of Stokes shift versus concentration was perceived as a point of change and was typically denoted as the CMC. All of the experiments were performed at room temperature.

Sucrose was purchased from J.T. Baker (USA). Cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), Triton X-100, sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Quinine sulfate was supplied by Alfa Aesar (Great Britain). Dodecyldimethyl(3-sulfopropyl)ammonium hydroxide (SB-12) was supplied by Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Japan). Ultrapure water from a Milli-Q Plus water purification system (18.2MΩ, Millipore, and Bedford, MA, USA) was used for all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and purification of fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots)
ce:section.ce:para: C-dots were prepared by controlled carbonization of sucrose modified method proposed by Minghong Wu group [27]. 2.5g of sucrose was weighed and dissolved by adding 2mL of deionized water. Add 3mL concentrated sulfuric acid dropwise into the solution with continuous vigorous stirring. The yellowish brown colored reaction mixture was neutralized with sufficient amount of NaOH. The dark brown color reaction mixture was isolated by a centrifuge operated at 18,000rpm for 10min. It was further purified by dialysis (MW cutoff 12–14kD) against Milli-Q water for 24h under constant stirring condition. In order to concentrate the amount of C-dots the solution was dried in a rotary vacuum evaporator (Büchi, Germany) and frozen dried for the quantification of C-dots.

The quantum yield (Q) of the C-dots has been calculated using the following equation, Eq. (1). Quinine sulfate (Q is 0.54) was chosen as a reference to determine Q, where Q denotes for quantum yield, I represents measured integrated emission intensity, n denotes the refractive index, and E is the optical density. R represents the fluorophore of known quantum yield for reference [28,29].
(1)
Q =
Q
R
I
I
R
E
R
E
n
2
n
R
2

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of critical micelle concentration of surfactants
ce:section.ce:para: 2mg of dried C-dots was mixed with varying concentrations of CTAB, SDS and Triton X-100 and make up to 5mL as the total volume. The stock solutions of all surfactants were prepared and used for further experiments. The concentrations of stock solutions for CTAB, SDS, Triton X-100, and SB-12 were 10mM, 50mM, 5mM, and 25mM. In case of CTAB, aqueous dilutions were prepared from 0.2 to 2mM at the interval of 0.2mM from a stock solution. As for SDS, 2–20mM diluted solutions were prepared with increment of 2mM from stock solution. In the case of Triton X-100, various dilutions prepared from 50 to 500µM with increment of 50µM from the stock solution. For SB-12, solutions were prepared from 0.5mM to 6.0mM with increment of 0.5mM from the stock solution. 2mg of highly purified C-dots was added in all the dilutions and make up the volume up to 5mL. These solutions were sonicated for 5min in order to equilibrate the solution. Similar procedure was followed for SDS except the dilutions were varied from 2 to 20mM from a stock of 50mM. 10mM Triton X-100 stock solution was prepared and 50 to 500µM dilutions were prepared from the stock solution. After equilibrating the C-dots-surfactants solution, the excitations as well as the emission spectra were recorded using the fluorescence spectroscope (Hitachi F 2700, Japan). The excitation maxima relative to emission maxima were recorded for each concentration of dilution sample.

The Stokes shift was calculated using the following equation:
(2)
Δ ν ( Stokesshift ) =
ν
ex
–
ν
em
where ν ex and ν em refer to the excitation and the emission maximum, respectively.
From the graphical representation of the Stokes shift versus concentration, CMC values of the surfactants were evaluated.

The morphology and uniformity of C-dots were measured by a high resolution transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Spectral properties of C-dots were studied by a UV–vis spectroscope (Thermo, Evolution 201, USA) and fluorescence spectroscope (Hitachi-2700, Japan) using a standard quartz cuvette having a path length of 1cm. Functional groups of C-dots were studied using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). The crystallinity of C-dots were studied using an X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Phillip, The Netherlands). Raman spectra were recorded from the Horiba, Jobin–Yvon Labram spectrometer with 630nm laser sources.

The brown colour C-dots solution exhibited intense green fluorescence strongly under UV light (λ ex=365nm), as shown in schematic illustration of Fig. 1. C-dots are spherical in shape and are highly mono-dispersed with uniform size ( Fig. 2A). Moreover, the TEM micrograph depicts the nanoscale size of C-dots with average particle diameter ranging from 3 to 6nm with maximum population of C-dots as 5nm (diameter), (see Fig. 2B). The UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 2C) of C-dots displayed continuous absorbance increasing from 280nm to 380nm which is in agreement with relevant literatures [27]. Fig. 2D showed the combined photoluminescence spectra of aqueous solution of C-dots with different excitation wavelengths. The aqueous C-dots solution was excited at 260–400nm with 20nm increment. The maximum intensity for emission spectra observed 387nm was excited at 280nm. The emission peak was shifted to a higher wavelength with the increase of the excitation wavelength. All experiments were carried out at 280nm (excitation wavelength) to determine the critical micelle concentration (CMC) for all surfactant-C-dots dilutions. An FTIR technique was used to identify the functional groups on the surface of C-dots. As shown in Fig. 3A, the band of the ester group appeared at 1719cm−1 and the peak of the C=O group found at 1719cm−1 depict aromatization. The broad peak at 3438cm−1 and the band in the range of 1000–1400cm−1 implied the existence of a large amount of hydroxyl groups and C-O groups. X-ray diffraction (XRD) of C-dots (Fig. 3B) reveals a characteristic peak at 2θ=20.12°, which can be assigned to the plane (002) with an interlayer spacing of 4.2Å, which is larger than that of bulk graphite (3.3Å), indicating poor crystallization [30]. The Raman spectrum shown in Fig. 3C reveals the typical graphitic nature of C-dots. C-dots exhibited D- and G-bands corresponding to 1345 and 1540cm−1 with no 2D peak in graphite. It can clearly be seen that the D peak is more prominent than G peak. This indicates the presence of amorphous carbon nanomaterials in the form of C-dots. The ratio of I
D /I
G , which is the characteristic peak of the disorder extent and the ratio of sp 3/sp 2 carbon, implying the extent of structural defects [30]. The I
D /I
G ratio of 2 indicates highly crystalline graphite. We calculated the I
D /I
G ratio for the as-synthesized C-dots and the estimated ratio of 0.873 indicating high purity of C-dots. The quantum yield of C-dots calculated with the reference to Quinine sulfate was found out to be 42%, which was summarized in Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The evaluation of critical micelle concentration (CMC) for CTAB, SDS, Triton X-100, and SB-12
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4A displays the impact of varying concentration of CTAB on the fluorescence properties of C-dots excited at 280nm, which is the preliminary criterion of the determination of CMC. Generally, cationic surfactants such as CTAB acquire positively charged head-groups that are partially neutralized by anions, while anionic surfactants possess negatively charged head groups that are partially neutralized by cations [31]. As CTAB concentration increases, there was consistent decrease in fluorescence intensity (emission) of C-dots (Fig. 4A). Another noteworthy finding was the comparable intensities of both emission and excitation spectra of C-dots-CTAB mixture. The red shift observed from 375nm to 425nm may be due to the surface protection of C-dots with CTAB which may affect the PL properties of C-dots. In the close proximity of miniature scale of C-dots in the solution, due to monomers of surfactants, many thermodynamically favoured reactions are initiated leading to the change of the surface as well as the optical properties of the materials. Due to the negative charge exhibited by the C-dots in aqueous solution [31], as the concentration of CTAB monomers increases, the interaction of C-dots with positively charged CTAB becomes more frequent and results in greater stability of the C-dots-surfactant aggregate and consequently causing larger Stokes shift [25]. The utilization of the Stokes shift for the evaluation of CMC is based on two key points: higher Stokes shift of monomers compared to micelles and the adsorption of CTAB molecules on C-dots. The surfactant molecules were scattered throughout the aqueous solution at lower concentration of CTAB (below CMC). As the concentration increased, the surfactant molecules began to aggregate at the air-solution interface. There is no sign of monomers remained after the CMC point and the Stokes shift decreased drastically which was shown in Fig. 4B. The capability of C-dots to exhibit such sharp transition reveals their suitability as an excellent molecular sensor for the present work. Due to this magnificent property observed in selected fluoro-probes, there is change in the emission as well as excitation spectrum and hence the considerable Stokes shift.

Fig. 4B explains the Stokes shift with respect to concentration of CTAB. It can be observed that, by virtue of pre-micellar concentration (concentration just before critical micelle concentration), the Stokes shift was found to be less with increasing concentration of CTAB. After a particular concentration of CTAB, there was a steep decrease in the Stokes shift and finally it levels off after a particular concentration indicating the accumulation of the CTAB monomers and this concentration is equivalent to CMC of CTAB. To calculate the critical micelle concentration (CMC), the lines on the plateau were extrapolated as shown in Fig. 4B. The point of intersection of the two lines was considered as the critical micelle concentration. The critical micelle concentration of CTAB was found to be 0.98mM which was in strong agreement with earlier findings [32].

Fig. 4C explains the impact of anionic surfactant such as sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) on PL properties of C-dots. In Fig. 4C, because of the considerable dissipating energy significant Stokes shift between excitation and emission was observed in this figure. This indicated that these Stokes shifts were found to be sensitive to SDS concentration. Since the electrostatic interactions exist predominantly between SDS and C-dots due to the negative charge of the surface head group of SDS, the Stokes shift increased rapidly when the concentration reached CMC at which circular pattern of SDS micelles formed despite the fact that very low Stokes shift of the monomers exhibited initially. This also indicates that very low energy fluorescence was observed for micelles as compared to that of monomers. Therefore, C-dots were unlikely remained and were saturated on micelles which showed nearly straight line in the figure as the concentrations higher than CMC. Fig. 4D displays the relation between the Stokes shift and the concentrations of the SDS. The sharp increase of Stokes shift from low value of SDS monomers to high value of micelles makes the point of change be clearly indicative of CMC value. As the concentration reached to CMC constant Stokes shift was observed from the surfactant and C-dots aggregate even with increased concentration of SDS. Using the aforementioned method, the CMC of SDS was determined to be 7.3mM which was in good agreement with the earlier findings.

In the case of non-ionic surfactant, Triton X-100 was considered as the model molecule for the CMC evaluation. Due to the absence of charge on the surface of head portion of the surfactant, weak interaction with C-dots was exhibited. Apart from this, the interactions of van der Waals force, steric repulsion or interface may play important roles in intermolecular forces. As shown in Fig. 4E, the minimum fluorescence range was observed from the PL spectra of Triton X-100 despite the change of concentration. Wide disparity in intensity between the excitation and emission spectra was clearly observed from Fig. 4E. Compared to cationic and anionic surfactants, non-ionic counterpart exhibits insignificant interaction between molecules and C-dots. Even with UV excitation a small Stokes shift was displayed demonstrates this weak interaction compare to the charged surfactants. Moreover, the weak fluorescence intensity shown compare to the charged counterparts of surfactants further confirms this hypothesis. As the surfactant is non-ionic the interactions with C-dots is prolonged and hence exhibited the different optical behavior. At low surfactant concentration very large excitation intensity was observed but the emission intensity was very low relatively. As the surfactant concentration increases, reverse excitation intensity was observed and the similar pattern occurred to emission. Fig. 4F demonstrates the relation between the Stokes shift and the concentration of Triton X-100; it reveals that monomers exhibited smaller Stokes shift compared to that of micelles. And very minute change in Stokes shift was observed even at CMC compared to ionic surfactants. The relationship between the variations of the Stokes shift in accordance with the alteration of surfactant concentration was plotted and the CMC value was indicated in Fig. 4F. As the concentration increased the Stokes shift was very small initially and sudden increase occurred as the concentration approached CMC and then the curve levelled off. As the fluorescence property of C-dots was negligibly influenced by the micelles of non-ionic surfactant Triton X-100 which was formed at CMC. Constant Stokes shift was observed above CMC as the concentration increased implying that insignificant change occurred in Stokes shift. Using the aforementioned method, the CMC of Triton X-100 was determined to be 0.19mM which was in good agreement of the earlier findings.

CMC determination of the zwitterionic surfactant was performed with the model molecule of dodecyldimethyl(3-sulfopropyl)ammonium hydroxide. Different concentrations of surfactant were prepared and were mixed with constant concentration of C-dots. From the measurements of the fluorescence pattern at a particular excitation wavelength of 340nm, the zwitterionic surfactant displayed distinct behavior. The fluorescence spectrum was recorded in Fig. 4G and the relationship of the Stokes shift vs concentration was demonstrated in Fig. 4H. Since the zwitterionic surfactant contain both cationic [+] and anionic [−] charges on different positions of the same molecule. From the mixing of the C-dots with the zwitterionic surfactant, the fluorescence pattern regarding the excitation wavelength exhibited unexpected consistency which is remarkably different from other surfactants. The maximum of excitation wavelength was fixed at the particular wavelength of 280nm for all concentrations. Just like other surfactants, the intensity of excitation varies. For greater clarity, the relationship between the Stokes shift, which was calculated from the energy difference of excitation and emission, and the surfactant concentration was illustrated in Fig. 4H. The Stokes shift decreased initially as concentration of surfactant increased, and as, the concentration reached to the particular concentration, namely CMC, the Stokes shift sharply increased and then proceeded to level off on a plateau. CMC of SB-12 was estimated to be 3.5mM according to the intersection of the extrapolation of curves in the graph of Stokes shift versus concentration in Fig. 4H. The close similarity of this Stokes shift correlation and those of anionic or non-ionic surfactants may result from the mild interaction of the zwitterionic surfactant with the cationic moiety of C-dots. For comparison, Table 2 presented some proposed protocols for CMC evaluation, including the results obtained in this work for CTAB, SDS, Triton X-100, and SB-12. It is clear that the results are in good agreement.

We have successfully developed a new and facile fluorophotometric approach for the determination of critical micelle concentration (CMC) of ionic and non-ionic surfactants with the assistance of C-dots. C-dots exhibited substantial fluorescence and therefore enhanced the sensitivity. The spectra were assigned and explained in terms of both electron transitions between specific molecular orbitals and the interaction with solvent. CMC values of cationic cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), anionic sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), nonionic Triton X-100, and zwitterionic dodecyldimethyl(3-sulfopropyl)ammonium hydroxide surfactant (SB-12) evaluated by this approach was found to be 0.98, 7.3, 0.19, and 3.5mM, respectively. The CMC values acquired were consistent with those obtained formerly by other methods. By introducing C-dots in the CMC evaluation of surfactants via fluorophotometric method opens new avenues for broader applications in the near future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan. The authors appreciate Mr. Bo-sgum Wu for his help in purchasing the chemicals. We thank the National Sun Yat-Sen University and Kaohsiung Medical University Joint Research Project (NSYSU-KMU JOINT RESEARCH PROJECT, #NSYSU-KMU (103-P 023) for financial support for this publication.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: J. Surf. Eng. Mater. Adv. Technol.
Spectrochim. Acta Part A: Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
